nativeRange.habitat: Can inhabit any reef habitat; tolerates a range of depths and light levels. Colonies have been documented in shallow lagoons and on reef slopes to approximately 50 m depth.

decorPreferences: stable base with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Like most reef corals, the Acanthastrea Coral relies primarily on its symbiotic zooxanthellae (photosynthetic algae within its tissue) for energy. Target (spot) feeding 1–2 times per week with meaty frozen foods — such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cyclops, and zooplankton — has been reported to benefit growth and coloration. Liquid and pellet foods have also been reported as accepted. Polyps extend feeding tentacles nocturnally.

avoidWith: other corals placed within sweeper range (3–7 cm)
other Acanthastrea colonies without buffer spacing

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x884. Sharp in-situ shot of a whole colony clearly showing the diagnostic 'starry cup' corallites with raised walls and dark polyp centers; best documentary framing (natural brown/tan wild coloration).
Debra Baker (via GBIF/iNaturalist); 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ colony from a different individual showing olive-tan fleshy polyps and corallite fringe; added for angle/color variety.

Will Thomas; 1443x1146. Vivid red-and-cream polyp macro illustrating the bright 'Acan' coloration hobbyists prize; kept as a colorful close-up extra (tight macro crop, shallow depth of field so not the recommended pick).

summary: The Acanthastrea Coral (Acanthastrea echinata), widely known as the 'Acan' in the reef hobby, is a large polyp stony coral found across the Indo-Pacific and into the western Indian Ocean. It is generally regarded as beginner-friendly, tolerating a range of light levels and rewarding weekly target feeding with vivid coloration.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note: The trade names 'Acan' and 'Acan Lord' historically referred to multiple species in the genus Acanthastrea. A 2016 reclassification split Acanthastrea lordhowensis into a separate genus as Micromussa lordhowensis — now sold as the 'Micro Lord' or 'Acan Lord' — and reduced the genus Acanthastrea to eight recognized species: A. echinata, A. bowerbanki, A. hillae, A. brevis, A. hemprichii, A. minuta, A. pachysepta, A. rotundoflora, and A. subechinata. A. echinata is now considered the primary 'true Acan' in the aquarium trade. A. hemprichii was additionally reclassified to Homophyllia australis in the same body of work. The aquarium trade still applies the name 'Acan' generically to both Acanthastrea species and Micromussa lordhowensis.

Lighting: Moderate light intensity is generally recommended, with a PAR range of roughly 75–200 being most commonly cited, and approximately 150 PAR considered a reasonable midpoint. Excessive intensity has been reported to damage tissue; newly acquired specimens are generally best acclimated by placing them lower in the tank and gradually moving them to their final position over 2–4 weeks.

Flow: Low to moderate, indirect flow is preferred. Direct high flow has been reported to cause polyp retraction and tissue tears. Gentle alternating flow tends to keep the coral clean without stressing tissue.

Placement: Middle to lower areas of the aquarium are generally advised, away from direct current impact and high-intensity light zones.

Aggression: Acanthastrea corals extend sweeper tentacles — thin, thread-like extensions used to sting neighboring corals — typically reaching 3–7 cm (approximately 1.5–3 inches) at night. A minimum buffer of 10 cm (about 4 inches) from other specimens is commonly advised, with more space recommended as the colony grows. Aggression has been noted even between two Acanthastrea colonies placed close together.

Feeding: Spot feeding 1–2 times per week is considered well-suited to this species. Meaty frozen foods target-fed via pipette or turkey baster are reported to promote growth and coloration. Although the species draws much of its energy from its symbiotic zooxanthellae (the photosynthetic algae living within its tissue), supplemental feeding appears to be beneficial. The Acanthastrea Coral is generally regarded as beginner-to-intermediate within the LPS category, given its hardiness relative to small polyp stony (SPS) corals — though this applies within a correctly cycled reef system with stable water chemistry.

Colony size: Wild colonies have been documented reaching approximately 1 m across. In home aquaria, typical growth produces colonies of 10–40 cm depending on tank size and conditions, with slow but steady expansion over years.

Colors: Wild specimens are often dull gray, brown, or green. Aquaculture and trade specimens frequently exhibit vivid multi-colored polyps — reds, oranges, greens, and blues — due to selective acquisition of colorful morphs. These designer color morphs are still commonly sold under the generic 'Acan' trade name.

Fragging: Colonies can be propagated by cutting (fragging) with bone cutters or a bandsaw. Frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs. Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity.

Do not confuse with Micromussa lordhowensis (formerly sold as 'Acan Lord'), which belongs to family Mussidae and is now classified under a separate genus. Both are generally regarded as beginner-friendly LPS corals with overlapping care requirements, but they are biologically distinct.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw, and the resulting fragments readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly practiced in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: ReefChasers – Acan Coral Care Guide (Acanthastrea echinata)

additional.name: Wikipedia – Acanthastrea echinata
additional.notes: Used for native range (western Indian Ocean through Pacific to southeastern Atlantic), depth range (to 50 m), colony size (up to ~1 m), and habitat description. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Acanthastrea (genus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order: Scleractinia, Family: Lobophylliidae), confirmed current 8-species genus composition following reclassification events. Confirmed A. lordhowensis is no longer in Acanthastrea.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Micromussa lordhowensis reclassification article
additional.notes: Used to document the Arrigoni et al. reclassification split: A. lordhowensis → Micromussa lordhowensis. Confirmed remaining Acanthastrea species include A. echinata, A. bowerbanki, and A. hillae (core trade-relevant species).

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Aquacultured Aussie Lord Frag (Micromussa lordhowensis)
additional.notes: Used for context on trade nomenclature overlap and to cross-reference care parameters for the former 'Acan Lord' (now Micromussa lordhowensis). Temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025. These parameters are for M. lordhowensis, not A. echinata; used only to confirm overlapping salinity/kH ranges. LiveAquaria carries no current A. echinata-specific product page.
